Pharmacists in Mental Health Care: Closing Psychiatric Pharmacotherapy Gaps

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
National Board of Medication Therapy Management

2,311 followers

2w
  • Report this post

The Role of Pharmacists in Mental Health Care (Part 1): Closing Psychiatric Pharmacotherapy Gaps It would be remiss not to address mental health given that May is Mental Health Awareness Month. Mental health needs continue to rise while workforce shortages persist across healthcare. Yet one important part of the solution is often underrecognized: Pharmacists. Psychiatric pharmacists and other pharmacists practicing within mental health settings are helping close critical care gaps through specialized expertise in psychiatric pharmacotherapy. Psychiatric medication management is rarely as simple as "prescribe and continue." Many patients require: • Careful medication selection • Monitoring for efficacy and adverse effects • Management of drug interactions • Titration and optimization • Addressing adherence challenges • Identification of medication-related causes of psychiatric symptoms • Education and shared decision-making This is where psychiatric pharmacists provide substantial value. Psychiatric pharmacists frequently collaborate with psychiatrists and interdisciplinary teams to: ✓ Recommend, interpret, and help operationalize pharmacogenomic testing when appropriate ✓ Facilitate evidence-based nonpharmacological interventions such as lifestyle and behavioral interventions, psychotherapy, and social or functional support ✓ Optimize psychiatric pharmacotherapy ✓ Manage pharmacotherapy-related adverse effects ✓ Support treatment-resistant conditions ✓ Monitor and manage metabolic risks associated with psychotropic medications ✓ Manage transitions of care ✓ Improve medication adherence and tolerability Beyond medication expertise, they often serve as translators between complex pharmacotherapy and patient understanding. We have an increasingly overburdened mental health system where access to psychiatrists and other mental health clinicians is limited, making pharmacist expertise even more valuable. But mental health care is not solely about expanding personnel numbers. It is also about maximizing the expertise of the professionals already embedded within our systems. For many patients, the difference between treatment failure and treatment success is not simply access to medication, but access to medication optimization. No mental health workforce discussion is complete without recognizing pharmaceutical care because medication optimization often influences the highly variable outcomes seen within this complex patient population. Pharmacists do not simply support mental health care. They expand its reach, strengthen its capacity, and are an important part of the solution. #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #PsychiatricPharmacy #MentalHealth #Pharmacists #HealthcareInnovation #PatientCare #BehavioralHealth #MedicationManagement

    🧠 ANTIPSYCHOTIC DRUGS: Understanding the Medication Behind Mental Health Recovery One of the most important lessons in psychiatric nursing is recognizing that antipsychotic medications do more than manage symptoms—they help individuals reconnect with reality, regain functioning, and improve quality of life. Antipsychotics primarily work by blocking dopamine (D2) receptors in the brain, helping reduce symptoms such as hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking. But effective patient care extends far beyond knowing the mechanism of action. 🔍 Key Clinical Insights Every Nurse Should Remember: ✅ First-Generation (Typical) Antipsychotics • Examples: Haloperidol, Chlorpromazine, Fluphenazine • Effective for positive psychotic symptoms • Higher risk of extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS) including dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, and tardive dyskinesia ✅ Second-Generation (Atypical) Antipsychotics • Examples: Risperidone, Olanzapine, Quetiapine, Clozapine • Lower incidence of EPS • Increased risk of metabolic complications such as weight gain, hyperglycemia, and dyslipidemia ⚠️ Critical Nursing Priorities • Monitor mental status and behavioral changes • Assess for EPS and tardive dyskinesia regularly • Monitor weight, BMI, blood glucose, and lipid levels • Watch for orthostatic hypotension and sedation • Recognize Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) immediately—fever, rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability require urgent intervention 💡 Patient Education Matters Patients should understand: ✔️ The importance of medication adherence ✔️ Not to discontinue medication abruptly ✔️ Early signs of adverse effects to report ✔️ Healthy lifestyle habits to reduce metabolic risks ✔️ The value of regular follow-up appointments As healthcare professionals, our role isn't only to administer medication—it is to educate, monitor, advocate, and support recovery throughout the patient's mental health journey. Every assessment, every conversation, and every intervention contributes to safer outcomes and better quality of life. ❤️ Mental health care is healthcare. What strategies have you found most effective for improving adherence and monitoring side effects in patients receiving antipsychotic therapy? #MentalHealth #PsychiatricNursing #NursingEducation #Pharmacology #Antipsychotics #PatientSafety #HealthcareProfessionals #ClinicalPractice #NurseLife #MentalHealthAwareness

    If you're an prescriber, therapist, or psychiatric provider, you may have already had this conversation A patient tells you they're exploring psilocybin-assisted therapy. Or ketamine. Or a psychedelic retreat program abroad. They're not asking for permission, they're going regardless. What they want is for you to be part of it, or at least offer a medical opinion on their proposed care plan. That puts providers in an uncomfortable position. Even in legal or licensed jurisdictions, the clinical guidance is still catching up. Liability concerns are real. Most are not trained to advise on something that sits this far outside conventional pathways. At the same time, there are risks to providers disengaging. Patients proceed without psychiatric oversight and medications aren't reviewed for interactions. No one is assessing readiness or flagging contraindications, and if the experience is destabilizing, which for some patients it can be, there's no coordinated response. For patients with straightforward histories, none of this may be a significant concern. Patients with complex medication regimens, treatment-resistant conditions, or significant trauma histories are another story. Continuity of care doesn't require you to endorse or facilitate psychedelic treatment. It requires staying in the picture, and having a clinical partner who can manage the areas that necessarily fall outside your scope. We work on a consulting basis with referring healthcare providers to create coordinated care structures around patients pursuing legal psychedelic services in compliant jurisdictions. That includes medication review, readiness assessment, risk stratification, preparation and integration support, and cross-provider communication when it's needed. Your patients are going to keep asking these questions. We can help you answer them and support the patients you'd otherwise have to watch navigate this alone. Reach out to learn more about provider collaboration.

    ACCEPTED 2026 WPA conference symposium 4000 - IMPROVING PSYCHIATRIC OUTCOMES IN PRIMARY CARE USING A COLLABORATIVE, EDUCATIONAL INTERVENTION: DESIGN, IMPLEMENTATION, AND DATA FROM CANADA AND SOMALILAND (ID 475) Chair(s): David A. Ross (Canada) and Djibril Moussa (Somaliland/USA) Session Description In many parts of the world, mental health care is severely limited by a shortage of psychiatrists (this may be true both in low- and middle-income countries and with rural and Indigenous communities in high-income countries). In these areas, most psychiatric treatment will, by necessity, occur in primary care settings that are not designed to meet the needs of these patients. To help mitigate the impact of this gap, we designed the Alberta Network for Community Health Outreach and Rural Mental Health (ANCHOR-MH), a novel educational program with the goals of: enhancing Family Medicine (FM) physicians’ confidence and skill at treating psychiatric illnesses; improving collaboration between FM physicians and psychiatrists; and establishing an enduring community of practice. Participants meet virtually for two hours each week (in addition to asynchronous engagement). The first hour consists of an innovative, experiential learning activity. To enhance knowledge translation, participants then have the opportunity for direct case consultation with both local psychiatrists and psychiatrists who specialize in rural care. In this session, we will begin with a brief introduction of the program and an illustration of the core teaching methods and session structure. We will share data from the first four cohorts in Alberta, Canada (n=20 each). We will then discuss how the program was adapted for use in Somaliland and share data from that group. Finally, we will reflect as a group on how system-level interventions may be used to support and empower primary care teams and to mitigate health disparities in underserved communities. Design and rationale for the Alberta Network for Community Health Outreach and Rural Mental Health (ANCHOR-MH) (ID 943) Presenter: David A. Ross (Canada) Implementation and iteration of ANCHOR-MH to improve rural and Indigenous mental health in Alberta, Canada (ID 931) Presenter: Jeremy Weleff (Canada) Qualitative and Quantitative data from first year of ANCHOR-MH (ID 932) Presenter: Evan Kyzar (United States of America) Adaptation and implementation of ANCHOR-MH in a Family Medicine residency program in Somaliland (ID 942) Presenter: Djibril Moussa (Somaliland)

    Sixty doctors joined our MedPsychKH webinar this morning. All MRCPsych-qualified. Several with years of NHS clinical experience. A dozen already practising as consultants or specialists in their own countries. A few hold PhDs from leading universities. Some with research portfolios, teaching credentials, international work in mental health. All sat in the non-prioritised category under the Medical Training (Prioritisation) Act 2026, and none secured an ST4 post for August 2026. I've run psychiatry career webinars reaching 150–200 doctors per session for over two years. This one was different. They didn't need portfolio coaching. They needed a map of where else they could go. I spent the weeks before talking to colleagues across industry, academia, NGOs, and senior NHS clinicians who'd taken non-traditional routes themselves. We worked through the picture for three groups of MRCPsych doctors: — IMG psychiatrists already in the UK on non-training contracts — Overseas MRCPsych doctors hoping to come to the UK — Specialists and consultants abroad who hold MRCPsych And we mapped how each group sits across the pathways open to them — UK clinical work and Portfolio Pathway, academic and PhD routes, pharma and digital mental health, global mental health and humanitarian work, consultant-equivalent roles overseas. Each group has different starting points, different friction, and different ceilings. What came through clearly is that "didn't get an ST4 seat" and "no career path" are not the same statement. Several of the attendees, by the end of the session, were openly considering routes they hadn't seriously thought about that morning. I'd love to hear from you if you'd be willing to share your experience to help others. Pharma medical affairs, digital health, NGO, academic, international, anything outside ST training. I would love to hear from you — I'll take the learning back to the doctors I'm working with. When one door closes, more windows open than most of us realise. The work is to show people where. #Psychiatry #MRCPsych #MedicalCareer

    The Role of Pharmacists in Mental Health Care (Part 2): Community Pharmacists, Long-Acting Injectables, and Expanding Access Mental health workforce shortages require creative solutions, and some of the strongest opportunities may already exist in neighborhoods across the country: Community pharmacies. Community pharmacists often have something uniquely powerful: Relationships. Patients may see their pharmacist monthly, sometimes more frequently than they see any other healthcare professional. Those interactions create familiarity, trust, and continuity. Increasingly, community pharmacists are leveraging these relationships to provide psychiatric long-acting injectable (LAI) services in collaboration with psychiatric and medical practices. Why does this matter? Psychiatric LAIs can improve: ✓ Medication adherence ✓ Continuity of treatment ✓ Relapse prevention ✓ Reduced hospitalization and readmission risk ✓ Potential reductions in healthcare utilization and costs ✓ Overall clinical stability Yet barriers frequently exist: • LAI patient hesitancy and needle phobia • Delays in transitioning from ineffective or insufficient oral therapy because of administration logistics or challenges communicating the benefits of LAI treatment • Transportation challenges • Limited psychiatric clinic capacity • Staffing shortages • Scheduling delays • Geographic access barriers Community pharmacy-based LAI programs can help bridge these gaps. Imagine the impact: A psychiatric provider evaluates and initiates therapy. The community pharmacist becomes an accessible extension of the care team by: • Reinforcing medication choices and discussing potential advantages with patients • Engaging in a reassuring, patient-centered LAI intake process • Administering scheduled injections • Monitoring for efficacy and adverse effects • Identifying adherence concerns • Managing missed doses and coordinating follow-up under collaborative practice agreements where applicable • Reinforcing patient education • Communicating updates, findings, and recommendations back to psychiatric practices Instead of creating parallel systems, this creates collaborative care ecosystems. Mental health access does not always require building new infrastructure. Sometimes it means activating resources already present in the community. Pharmacists are increasingly demonstrating that they are not simply dispensers of medication. They are highly accessible points for care. As workforce shortages continue, pharmacist-led psychiatric LAI models may become one of healthcare's most practical and scalable solutions. Pharmacists do not simply support mental health care. They expand its reach, strengthen its capacity, and are an important part of the solution. #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #BehavioralHealth #MentalHealth #PsychiatricPharmacy #CommunityPharmacy #LongActingInjectables #HealthcareInnovation #PatientCare

    “Can you believe we spent 40 minutes on a prior authorization for Ozempic today?” A physician said this during a conversation recently. Not with a difficult patient. Not during a complicated procedure. Just… trying to get a medication approved. Meanwhile: Patients were waiting. Phones were ringing. The schedule kept moving. And the frustrating part? The treatment plan was already decided. The delay wasn’t clinical. It was administrative. This is one of the biggest operational drains I keep hearing about across U.S. practices: Prior authorizations consuming time that should be spent on patient care. Especially in: Psychiatry Internal medicine Pain management Specialty care The challenge isn’t just the paperwork. It’s the constant back-and-forth: • Insurance follow-ups • Missing documentation • Status checks • Denials and resubmissions And over time… It creates friction everywhere: Staff burnout Delayed treatment Frustrated patients Slower workflows Most people outside healthcare think delays happen inside the exam room. But often… They happen after the visit is already over. How much time does your practice realistically lose each week dealing with prior authorizations?

    Psychiatry has become the largest collaboration category on the Single Aim marketplace: accounting for 6 of every 10 collaboration requests, more than every other specialty combined. In high-demand states like Alabama, Mississippi, and Georgia, a meaningful share of these roles receive no psychiatrist responses at all. Demand is outpacing supply. Two patterns from the marketplace data: 1. 94% of psych NP practices seeking collaboration are telehealth or hybrid. The collaborating psychiatrist role has shifted from local clinic supervisor to remote oversight relationship. 2. The supply gap is concentrated in states with the strictest collaboration rules: proximity requirements, in-person meeting mandates, chart review minimums. Regulation is the binding constraint, not psychiatrist interest. Our new guide covers pay by state, where the supply gaps are, how state rules shape the role, and what psychiatrists should evaluate before signing. https://lnkd.in/g2vhzdEF

    As Kevin Pho, M.D., highlights, primary care clinicians are often the first (and sometimes only) point of contact for patients experiencing mental health concerns. This makes effective, evidence-based mental health training for PCPs a vital step towards increasing access and meeting the needs of children and families across the country.

    Kevin Pho, M.D. Kevin Pho, M.D. is an Influencer

    Physician | KevinMD.com | The Podcast by KevinMD

    2w

    Seventy-five percent of all mental health care in this country is handled by primary care doctors. The total amount of training they receive in mental health, across four years of medical school and up to five years of residency, is 2 percent.   This is not a story about individual physicians failing their patients. It is a story about a training system that has not changed in a hundred years producing doctors who were never taught what they most need to know.   Robert C. Smith, MD, an internal medicine physician and author of Has Medicine Lost Its Mind, conducted randomized controlled trials that identified the first evidence-based patient-centered interviewing method and the first primary care mental health model. He knows what effective training looks like. The system has not adopted it.   For anyone who has sat across from a patient in distress, or who manages people who do, and known the training was never there, the numbers explain why.   First. Seventy-five percent of mental health care is handled by primary care doctors with 2 percent of total training. The gap is not a resource problem. It is a curriculum problem that has not changed since Flexner.   Second. There are 48,000 suicides per year, half of whom saw their doctor in the preceding two to four weeks. There are 15,000 opioid overdose deaths per year prescribed by untrained physicians. These are not clinical failures. They are training failures.   Third. Seventeen percent of chronic disease patients carry an untreated major mental disorder. When it goes unrecognized, recurrent hospitalizations and worsening physical outcomes follow. The cost is not abstract. It is the $5 trillion health expenditure that could be cut by trillions if preventable conditions were prevented.   Fourth. Medicine's leadership, the AAMC, ACGME, and AMA, has known about the bio-psychosocial model since Engel proposed it in the 1970s. Fifty years of lip service. Training has not changed.   Smith's proposed remedy is public pressure, the Nader and Carson model. Unsafe at Any Speed gave us seat belts. Silent Spring gave us the EPA. Has Medicine Lost Its Mind seeks to do the same for mental health training.   "It's not your fault. You have not been trained."   Search "The Podcast by KevinMD" wherever you listen to podcasts.   What is the single change to medical training that would most reduce the gap between the mental health care doctors are expected to provide and the training they actually receive?   #HealthcareLeadership #MentalHealthTraining #PhysicianAdvocacy #MedicalEducation #ThePodcastbyKevinMD

    BIG NEWS! The Therapeutic Goods Administration has just released more flexible requirements for the delivery of MDMA and psilocybin assisted therapies for patients suffering from post-traumatic stress disorder and patients suffering from treatment resistant depression. This is something that Mind Medicine Australia has been advocating for with the TGA since early 2023 when the TGA announced its initial requirements. Importantly, the TGA’s more flexible requirements should improve patient access and reduce patient costs without adversely affecting patient safety. The changes include: 1. Broadening the range of therapists who are able to be involved in delivering psychedelic assisted therapy. The TGA had been insisting that a clinical psychologist had to be present with the patient throughout the dosing sessions in the absence of the prescribing psychiatrist. Given the shortages of both psychiatrists and clinical psychologists this requirement was adversely affecting patient access and significantly increasing patient costs. The TGA has now expanded the clinical psychologist requirement to include general practitioners, mental health nurses and occupational therapist who are registered with their appropriate National Boards. There is also broader scope for the prescribing psychiatrist to choose the background of the other therapist provided the therapist has appropriate training in these therapies and experience with treating patients with the relevant mental health conditions. 2. Confirming that whilst the psychiatrist needs to be present for the actual administration of the medicines the psychiatrist doesn’t need to be in the room with the patient for the remainder of the dosing session and can be offsite but available. 3. Confirmation that the dosing sessions can occur in a clinic rather than a hospital provided that the clinic is within 15 minutes of an emergency department. This will all lead to more accessible therapy teams, easier clinical logistics, greater patient accessibility and lower patient costs. The one critical area that the TGA didn’t sort out was minimum clinical practitioner training requirements. The TGA argued that it didn’t have the legislative mandate to do this but it does represent by far the weakest link in the regulatory requirements as it directly impacts patient safety. Mind Medicine Australia’s Certificate in Psychedelic Assisted Therapies (CPAT) course is taught by leading experts in the field from around the World and involves over 100 hours of structured learning including a one week residential. We are now seeing some courses being run over a weekend which can’t possibly provide practitioners with adequate safety training in this merging field. Mind Medicine Australia’s submission to the TGA can be found here – https://buff.ly/NNxEQmu The full TGA announcement can be found here https://buff.ly/fjuG1Ae
